title: Securing Your Linux Server: Essential First Steps
description: Harden a fresh Ubuntu 24.04 server by creating a non-root user, locking down SSH, configuring a firewall with UFW, and setting up fail2ban to block brute-force attacks.
prerequisites: A running Ubuntu 24.04 server (see exercise 1: Creating and Connecting to Your First Linux Server)
SSH access as root to your server
learningObjectives: Create a non-root user with sudo privileges
Disable root login and password authentication over SSH
Configure UFW to allow only necessary traffic
Install and configure fail2ban to block repeated login attempts
Verify that security settings are working correctly

sponsorCta.text: You need a running Linux server for this exercise. Any Ubuntu server works, but we recommend DigitalOcean for quick setup.

steps.title: Create a Non-Root User with Sudo Access
steps.description: Running everything as root is risky. A single typo can destroy your system. Create a regular user and give it sudo privileges so you can run admin commands only when needed.
steps.expectedOutput:
Adding user `deploy' ...
Adding new group `deploy' (1001) ...
Adding new user `deploy' (1001) with group `deploy' ...
Creating home directory `/home/deploy' ...
Copying files from `/etc/skel' ...
New password:
Retype new password:
passwd: password updated successfully
...
uid=1001(deploy) gid=1001(deploy) groups=1001(deploy),27(sudo)
hints: Choose a strong password for the new user
The -aG flag appends the user to the sudo group without removing existing groups
You can use any username you want instead of 'deploy'
validationCriteria: User 'deploy' exists on the system
User is a member of the sudo group
id command shows sudo (group 27) in the output

steps.title: Copy SSH Key to the New User
steps.description: Copy your SSH public key from the root account to the new user so you can log in directly without a password.
steps.expectedOutput:
No output means success. Verify with:
ls -la /home/deploy/.ssh/
total 12
drwx------ 2 deploy deploy 4096 ... .
drwxr-xr-x 4 deploy deploy 4096 ... ..
-rw------- 1 deploy deploy  XXX ... authorized_keys
hints: The .ssh directory must be 700 and authorized_keys must be 600, or SSH will refuse to use them
chown -R changes ownership recursively for the directory and all files inside it
Test the new user login from your local machine: ssh deploy@YOUR_DROPLET_IP
validationCriteria: The file /home/deploy/.ssh/authorized_keys exists
Permissions on .ssh are 700
Permissions on authorized_keys are 600
You can SSH in as the deploy user from your local machine

steps.title: Test Sudo Access for the New User
steps.description: Open a new terminal window and SSH in as the deploy user. Verify that sudo works before locking down root access.
hints: You will be prompted for the deploy user's password when running sudo for the first time
If sudo fails, go back to the root session and verify the user is in the sudo group
Keep your root SSH session open until you confirm the deploy user works
validationCriteria: sudo whoami returns 'root'
You can read protected files with sudo
The deploy user is confirmed working before proceeding

steps.title: Disable Root Login and Password Authentication
steps.description: Now that you have a working sudo user, disable root SSH login and password authentication. This blocks the two most common attack vectors on Linux servers.
hints: Always verify the grep output before restarting SSH
Do NOT close your current SSH session until you test logging in from a new terminal
If you lock yourself out, use the DigitalOcean console from the dashboard to fix it
On Ubuntu 24.04, the service is called 'ssh', not 'sshd'
validationCriteria: PermitRootLogin is set to 'no'
PasswordAuthentication is set to 'no'
SSH service restarts without errors
You can still log in as the deploy user from a new terminal
Root login is rejected from a new terminal

steps.title: Configure UFW Firewall
steps.description: UFW (Uncomplicated Firewall) is the default firewall tool on Ubuntu. Set it up to allow only SSH, HTTP, and HTTPS traffic, and block everything else.
steps.expectedOutput:
Default incoming policy changed to 'deny'
Default outgoing policy changed to 'allow'
Rule added
Rule added (v6)
Rule added
Rule added (v6)
Rule added
Rule added (v6)
Firewall is active and enabled on system startup

Status: active
Logging: on (low)
Default: deny (incoming), allow (outgoing), disabled (routed)
New profiles: skip

To                         Action      From
--                         ------      ----
22/tcp                     ALLOW IN    Anywhere                   # SSH
80/tcp                     ALLOW IN    Anywhere                   # HTTP
443/tcp                    ALLOW IN    Anywhere                   # HTTPS
22/tcp (v6)                ALLOW IN    Anywhere (v6)              # SSH
80/tcp (v6)                ALLOW IN    Anywhere (v6)              # HTTP
443/tcp (v6)               ALLOW IN    Anywhere (v6)              # HTTPS
hints: Always allow SSH (port 22) BEFORE enabling the firewall, or you will lock yourself out
The --force flag skips the confirmation prompt
You can add comments to rules so you remember why they were created
If you need to remove a rule later: sudo ufw delete allow 80/tcp
validationCriteria: UFW is active and enabled on boot
Default incoming policy is deny
SSH (22), HTTP (80), and HTTPS (443) are allowed
You are still connected via SSH after enabling the firewall

steps.title: Install and Configure fail2ban
steps.description: fail2ban monitors log files for repeated failed login attempts and temporarily bans the offending IP addresses. It is one of the best defenses against brute-force SSH attacks.
commands:
sudo tee /etc/fail2ban/jail.d/sshd.conf << 'EOF'
[sshd]
enabled = true
port = ssh
filter = sshd
logpath = /var/log/auth.log
maxretry = 5
bantime = 3600
findtime = 600
EOF
steps.expectedOutput:
Reading package lists... Done
...
Setting up fail2ban ...

Synchronizing state of fail2ban.service...
Created symlink /etc/systemd/system/multi-user.target.wants/fail2ban.service ...
hints: Never edit jail.conf directly; always use jail.local or files in jail.d/
maxretry = 5 means an IP gets banned after 5 failed attempts
bantime = 3600 bans the IP for 1 hour (3600 seconds)
findtime = 600 means the 5 failed attempts must happen within 10 minutes
validationCriteria: fail2ban is installed and running
The sshd jail is enabled
fail2ban is set to start on boot

steps.title: Verify fail2ban is Working
steps.description: Check that fail2ban is running, the SSH jail is active, and review the current ban status.
steps.expectedOutput:
Status
|- Number of jail:      1
`- Jail list:   sshd

Status for the jail: sshd
|- Filter
|  |- Currently failed: 0
|  |- Total failed:     0
|  `- Journal matches:  _SYSTEMD_UNIT=ssh.service + _COMM=sshd
`- Actions
   |- Currently banned: 0
   |- Total banned:     0
   `- Banned IP list:

3600
5
hints: The 'Currently banned' count will increase as bots try to brute-force your server
To manually unban an IP: sudo fail2ban-client set sshd unbanip IP_ADDRESS
Check /var/log/fail2ban.log to see ban/unban activity
validationCriteria: fail2ban status shows the sshd jail is active
Ban time and max retry values match your configuration
No errors in the fail2ban output

steps.title: Run a Final Security Check
steps.description: Verify that all security settings are in place by running a series of checks.
steps.expectedOutput:
permitrootlogin no
passwordauthentication no

Status: active
To                         Action      From
--                         ------      ----
22/tcp                     ALLOW       Anywhere
80/tcp                     ALLOW       Anywhere
443/tcp                    ALLOW       Anywhere
...

enabled

LISTEN 0      128          0.0.0.0:22        0.0.0.0:*    users:(("sshd",pid=...,fd=3))
hints: sshd -T shows the active SSH configuration, which is more reliable than reading the config file
ss -tlnp shows listening TCP ports and the processes using them
If any check fails, go back and review the corresponding step
validationCriteria: Root login is disabled
Password authentication is disabled
UFW is active with the correct rules
fail2ban is enabled
Only expected ports are listening

completionCriteria: A non-root user with sudo privileges can log in via SSH
UFW firewall is active and allows only SSH, HTTP, and HTTPS
fail2ban is running and monitoring SSH login attempts

resources.title: UFW Essentials: Common Firewall Rules and Commands

troubleshooting.issue: Locked out of the server after disabling root login
troubleshooting.solution: Use the DigitalOcean console (in the dashboard under your droplet, click 'Access' then 'Launch Droplet Console') to log in as root and fix the SSH config. Then re-test your deploy user login.

troubleshooting.issue: UFW blocks SSH and you lose connection
troubleshooting.solution: Use the DigitalOcean console to log in and run 'sudo ufw allow 22/tcp' followed by 'sudo ufw reload'. Always allow SSH before enabling the firewall.

troubleshooting.issue: fail2ban does not start or shows errors
troubleshooting.solution: Check the logs with 'sudo journalctl -u fail2ban -n 50'. A common problem is syntax errors in the jail config. Verify your config with 'sudo fail2ban-client -t'.
